mainText:
**Utah’s Eternal Orchard**
By Eric Jay Toll

An armload of apricots made descending the ladder a little shaky for a woman. I watched from the fence as she handed them to her companion to drop into the bag. As she turned in the deep shade of the orchard, she came face-to-face with a grazing mule deer.

On this bright, sunny afternoon, stepping into the shade of the apricot trees, I easily pictured a summer in the 1880s with pioneer Merin Smith, his family, and neighbors climbing ladders, poking branches with long poles, and collecting fruit in wooden bushel baskets from these same trees. This orchard is one of the larger of the 19 pioneer orchards in Fruita, Utah, which is part of Capitol Reef National Park. Once one of America’s most isolated communities, it still retains that charm today.

Capitol Reef was named a national monument in 1937 and designated a national park in 1971. It is named for the sandstone domes atop its red rock canyon walls, shaped by wind, rain, snow, and ice. Fruita’s historic importance lies not only in the former homes of the 10 or so pioneer families that settled there, but in its nearly 150-year-old orchards, which still produce fruit that visitors can pick for $2 a pound today.

In the 1880s, Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints leader Brigham Young called pioneers to settle the lands of Zion—today’s Utah and portions of Arizona, Nevada, and California. One small group of families threaded their carts through southern Utah’s slot canyons and narrows. Exiting Capitol Gorge, the settlers found a paradise along the Fremont River, nestled between steep red rock canyon walls and lush with native grasses and trees.

The pioneers from the LDS church settled into this weather-protected valley, which had once been home to indigenous tribes for thousands of years. According to Shauna Cottrell, Chief Resource Ranger for Capitol Reef, Fruita was one of the last places settled and mapped in the lower 48 states because it is challenging to access.

Before World War II, there were only two roads into Fruita. The most accessible one, leading from Torrey, west of Fruita, was not paved until 1952. That same road, now Utah Highway 24, didn’t cut through the park to the east until 10 years later. Before that, the east road was a rugged, rocky wagon trail through Capitol Gorge, which could be blocked by flooding or falling boulders from the canyon walls. Today, Capitol Gorge is not drivable but serves as a popular hiking trail.

Life in Fruita changed when President Franklin D. Roosevelt designated Capitol Reef a national monument. Along with regular fruit buyers from nearby communities, Fruita began attracting visitors to explore the new monument. As tourism increased, families of the original settlers moved out to larger towns seeking greater job opportunities, allowing the Park Service to buy out the remaining homes.

As the tourism boom continued, Dewey and Nell Gifford built several cabins behind their home and operated a motel. Nell Gifford made small pies from local fruit to serve her guests, a tradition that has continued. Last year, the bakery counter at the Gifford House and Museum sold 81,000 pies.

The Giffords were the last of the Fruita families to sell to the Park Service, leaving their home in 1969 after 40 years, effectively ending the community’s life as a pioneer village. Two years later, the monument officially became a national park.

Coming from the cool of the shady picnic area at the Ripple Rock Nature Center, I crossed Scenic Drive and peered into the small barn-like blacksmith shop. There, I saw farming artifacts from the community ranging from the 1880s to the 1960s, with charred beams over the smithy’s firepit and a tool-stocked workbench.

Just east of the visitor center on Utah 24, across from the Smith orchard, the one-room log schoolhouse sits at the base of the red rock cliff. Inside, desks and chalkboards await students who will never return. The schoolhouse served as the community center, a gathering spot for festivities, and the LDS ward house for the pioneers calling Fruita home.

Returning to the car from the orchard, one can easily picture the old community: a place where people helped each other in this isolated desert paradise of the Colorado Plateau.
